content:
# Joshua Chapter 23 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), folio pages 247-248
**Source Image:** `JOS_023_page_001.jpg`, `JOS_023_page_002.jpg`

---

**23:1** And it was, after many days, when YHWH had given rest to Israel from all their enemies round about, and Joshua had grown old, advanced in days,

**23:2** that Joshua summoned all Israel — their elders, and their heads, and their judges, and their officers — and said to them, "I have grown old, I am advanced in days.

**23:3** And you yourselves have seen all that YHWH your God has done to all these nations before you, for YHWH your God is the one who has been fighting for you.

**23:4** See, I have allotted to you these remaining nations as an inheritance for your tribes, from the Jordan, and all the nations that I have cut off, to the Great Sea toward the setting of the sun.

**23:5** And YHWH your God — he shall thrust them out from before you and shall drive them out from before you, and you shall possess their land, just as YHWH your God spoke to you.

**23:6** And you shall be very strong to keep and to do all that is written in the scroll of the Torah of Moses, not turning from it to the right or to the left,

**23:7** so as not to come among these nations, these remaining ones with you. And the name of their gods you shall not invoke, and you shall not swear by them, and you shall not serve them, and you shall not bow down to them.

**23:8** But to YHWH your God you shall cling, just as you have done to this day.

**23:9** And YHWH has driven out from before you great and mighty nations, and as for you — no man has stood before you to this day.

**23:10** One man of you shall put a thousand to flight, for YHWH your God is the one who fights for you, just as he spoke to you.

**23:11** And you shall guard your souls diligently to love YHWH your God.

**23:12** For if turning, you turn back, and you cling to the remnant of these nations — these remaining ones with you — and you intermarry with them, and you come among them and they among you,

**23:13** knowing, you shall know that YHWH your God will not continue to drive out these nations from before you. And they shall become for you a snare and a trap, and a scourge on your sides, and thorns in your eyes, until you perish from upon this good land that YHWH your God has given you.

**23:14** And behold, I am going today the way of all the earth. And you know with all your heart and with all your soul that not one word has fallen from all the good words that YHWH your God spoke concerning you. All have come to pass for you; not one word of them has fallen.

**23:15** And it shall be, just as every good word has come upon you that YHWH your God spoke to you, so shall YHWH bring upon you every evil word, until he has destroyed you from upon this good land that YHWH your God has given you —

**23:16** when you transgress the covenant of YHWH your God that he commanded you, and you go and serve other gods, and you bow down to them. Then the anger of YHWH shall burn against you, and you shall perish quickly from upon the good land that he has given you."

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Method
Each verse was compared across all three translations (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) and checked against the manuscript images of Leningrad Codex pages 247-248. Where all three translators agreed on a reading, that agreement was adopted. Where they diverged, the manuscript was consulted and the wisest synthesis was chosen, favoring accuracy to the Hebrew while maintaining readable English prose.

### Agreements
The three translators demonstrated strong agreement across Joshua 23. All three preserve:
- **YHWH** (not "the LORD") throughout all sixteen verses
- **The fourfold leadership address** in v. 2 (elders, heads, judges, officers)
- **The Great Sea** as the name for the Mediterranean in v. 4
- **The four prohibitions** regarding foreign gods in v. 7 (invoke/swear/serve/bow)
- **The verb "cling"** (davaq) for covenantal attachment to YHWH in v. 8
- **The fourfold consequence imagery** in v. 13 (snare, trap, scourge/whip, thorns)
- **"The way of all the earth"** as Joshua's death euphemism in v. 14
- **The symmetry of blessing and curse** in vv. 14-15 (good word fulfilled, evil word likewise)
- **"The anger of YHWH shall burn"** in v. 16

### Major Decisions

**1. "Advanced in days" vs. "well along in years" (vv. 1-2)**
EMET and LOGOS preserve the Hebrew idiom ba ba-yamim as "advanced in days." PNEUMA renders it "well along in years." The SOPHIA consensus adopts "advanced in days" (EMET, LOGOS) because the idiom is comprehensible in English and preserves the characteristic Hebrew phrase that recurs as a marker of patriarchal/leadership transitions. See Disagreement Log.

**2. "Summoned" vs. "called" (v. 2)**
EMET renders va-yiqra as "called," while LOGOS renders "summoned" and PNEUMA "called together." SOPHIA adopts "summoned" (LOGOS), which best conveys the formal, deliberate gathering of national leadership for a farewell address. See Disagreement Log.

**3. "Has been fighting" vs. "fought" vs. "is the one fighting" (v. 3)**
EMET uses the present participle "the one fighting," LOGOS the past tense "who fought," and PNEUMA "who has been fighting." The Hebrew ha-nilcham is a Niphal participle, expressing ongoing or characteristic action. SOPHIA adopts "who has been fighting" (PNEUMA's tense, combined with EMET/LOGOS structure), capturing the participial aspect — YHWH's warfare on Israel's behalf has been continuous, not a single past event. See Disagreement Log.

**4. "Scroll" vs. "book" of the Torah of Moses (v. 6)**
EMET and PNEUMA render sefer as "scroll," while LOGOS renders "book." SOPHIA adopts "scroll" (EMET, PNEUMA) because it reflects the actual medium of the text in Joshua's era. LOGOS's note on the key phrase "book of the Torah of Moses" is acknowledged, but "scroll" is historically accurate and avoids anachronism. See Disagreement Log.

**5. "Torah" vs. "Instruction" of Moses (v. 6)**
EMET and LOGOS retain "Torah," while PNEUMA renders torah as "Instruction." SOPHIA retains "Torah" (EMET, LOGOS) as a loanword that carries the full semantic range of the Hebrew — instruction, teaching, law, direction — without reducing it to any single English equivalent. See Disagreement Log.

**6. "Not turning from it to the right or to the left" vs. "never straying from it one way or the other" (v. 6)**
EMET and LOGOS preserve the Hebrew spatial idiom "right or left," while PNEUMA paraphrases as "one way or the other." SOPHIA preserves the Hebrew idiom (EMET, LOGOS), which is a recognizable biblical formula for total fidelity and is readily understood in English. See Disagreement Log.

**7. "Invoke" vs. "bring to remembrance" (v. 7)**
EMET renders tazkiru as "bring to remembrance," LOGOS as "invoke," and PNEUMA as "invoke." SOPHIA adopts "invoke" (LOGOS, PNEUMA), which captures the Hiphil causative sense (causing the name to be remembered/spoken) in a single word that conveys both the speech-act and its ritual significance. See Disagreement Log.

**8. "Guard your souls diligently" vs. "be very careful" (v. 11)**
EMET renders "guard your souls very much," LOGOS "guard your souls diligently," and PNEUMA "be very careful... with your whole being." SOPHIA adopts "guard your souls diligently" (LOGOS), preserving both nefesh ("souls" in the Hebrew holistic sense of "your whole selves") and the intensity of me'od, while reading smoothly. See Disagreement Log.

**9. Infinitive absolute: "turning, you turn back" (v. 12)**
EMET preserves the infinitive absolute construction shov tashuvu as "indeed turn back." LOGOS renders "turn back at all," and PNEUMA "ever turn away." SOPHIA renders "turning, you turn back" to preserve the characteristic Hebrew doubling construction per project convention for infinitive absolute forms. See Disagreement Log.

**10. Infinitive absolute: "knowing, you shall know" (v. 13)**
EMET preserves the infinitive absolute yado'a ted'u as "knowing, you shall know." LOGOS renders "know for certain," and PNEUMA "know for certain." SOPHIA adopts "knowing, you shall know" (EMET), preserving the infinitive absolute doubling construction that mirrors v. 12 and creates the emphatic cadence of Joshua's solemn warning. See Disagreement Log.

**11. "Has fallen" vs. "has failed" (v. 14)**
EMET renders nafal as "has fallen," LOGOS and PNEUMA as "has failed." The Hebrew verb is literally "to fall." SOPHIA adopts "has fallen" (EMET) for the first occurrence and retains it for the repeated statement at the end of the verse, preserving the concrete Hebrew image. The verb nafal is deliberate — words "fall" to the ground unfulfilled, or they stand. The metaphor is vivid and comprehensible. See Disagreement Log.

**12. "With all your heart and with all your soul" vs. "with every fiber of your being" (v. 14)**
EMET and LOGOS preserve the Hebrew doublet "with all your heart and with all your soul" (be-khol levavkhem u-ve-khol nafshekem). PNEUMA consolidates to "with every fiber of your being." SOPHIA preserves the full doublet (EMET, LOGOS), as this is a recognizable covenant formula echoing the Shema (Deut 6:5), and collapsing it erases an important intertextual link. See Disagreement Log.

**13. "Every evil word" vs. "every disaster he warned of" (v. 15)**
EMET and LOGOS preserve the Hebrew kol ha-davar ha-ra as "every evil word," while PNEUMA renders "every disaster he warned of." SOPHIA adopts "every evil word" (EMET, LOGOS) to preserve the deliberate parallelism with "every good word" in the same verse, which is a key rhetorical and theological structure in Joshua's speech. See Disagreement Log.

**14. "Destroyed" vs. "wiped out" (v. 15)**
EMET and LOGOS render hashmid as "destroyed," while PNEUMA renders "wiped you out." SOPHIA adopts "destroyed" (EMET, LOGOS), the standard rendering of the Hiphil of shamad, which carries the full force of covenantal annihilation without colloquial overtones. See Disagreement Log.

### Manuscript Observations
The Leningrad Codex pages 247-248 confirm the Masoretic text as transmitted. Joshua 23 appears in the standard three-column format with full vocalization and cantillation marks. Notable observations:
- The parashah markers are visible, confirming the chapter's structure as a unified farewell discourse.
- The divine name (YHWH) with its distinctive four-letter form is clearly legible throughout the passage, confirming its frequent repetition in this chapter (fourteen occurrences).
- The infinitive absolute constructions in vv. 12-13 (shov tashuvu, yado'a ted'u) are clearly written with the expected vowel pointing, confirming the emphatic doubling that SOPHIA preserves.
- No textual variants or scribal corrections are evident in the manuscript images for this chapter.

---
